item.title: Street Trees in NYC
item.dc:creator: Connor Wolosewicz
item.description:
Environmental Justice in NYC: Down to the (Street) Tree.

Does Street Tree Health Differ by Borough in NYC?

For this analysis, in my continuing studies of urban sustainability, I wanted to understand environmental equity in NYC on a more granular basis than parks. This data comes from the 2015 Street Trees Census, is much larger (600,000+) than the previous one, and will likely be dwarfed once that 2025 census comes out. It includes a unique ID for each tree, both the common and scientific names, which block it is located, the diameter at breast heigh (DBH), and more. This dataset is available to the public here.

Variables

In this dataset, one row represents a tree. I completed a hypothesis test between two groups: health and borough. I aim to see if whether the proportion of trees in GOOD health differ between the richest borough, Manhattan, and the poorest, The Bronx.

Variable Type Description
HEALTH Categorical Rated on a scale of GOOD, FAIR, or POOR
BOROUGH Categorical NYC borough where the park is located (1 = Manhattan, 2 = Bronx)

Exploratory Comparison

To get a better glimpse of each borough’s urban canopy health, I first cleaned the data and then grouped it by BOROUGH and HEALTH. Upon further inspection, the boroughs had vastly different tree counts. Manhattan is home to 62,416 trees compared to Bronx’s 80,557. To better compare the two, I mutated the data into a proportionally comparison so that they are on a 0-1 scale.

At first glance, the proportion of trees in different health categories appears similar, with the vast majority in both boroughs being labeled GOOD. However, a more exact measure from the summary data shows that Manhattan has a higher proportion of both FAIR and POOR trees—18.4% and 5.8%, respectively—compared to The Bronx at 13.5% and 3.8%. This surprising, given that Manhattan has more income and in theory, more funding to keep trees healthy.

95% Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Proportion of Good Health Trees
Borough Observed Proportion Lower CI Upper CI
Bronx 0.826 0.824 0.829
Manhattan 0.759 0.755 0.762

For this analysis, box plots were not used because HEALTH is a categorical, not continuous, variable. Instead, a grouped bar chart of proportions better reflects the health distributions across the two boroughs. Thus, there are not outliers in the traditional sense; yet, it is clear that The Bronx has a noticeably higher proportion of GOOD trees.

Bootstrapping

In order to get a better understanding of how different the two boroughs are, I constructed a bootstrap confidence interval (CI) for each. As seen in the graph, the 95% CI does not overlap at all, and indeed, there is a difference of trees in GOOD health. Specifically, the CI for The Bronx is between 82.4% and 82.9%, with Manhattan’s at 75.5% and 76.2%. The difference between the lower bound of The Bronx and the upper bound of Manhattan is just over 6 percentage points. This provides strong visual and statistical evidence that the difference in tree health is not attributable to random chance.

Note: The graph is zoomed for comparison purposes.

Hypotheses

For my statistical analysis, Group 1 is The Bronx and Group 2 is Manhattan. The null hypothesis—what I am looking to disprove—states that the proportion of trees in GOOD health will be the same across both groups, ad any observed difference is due to random chance. Alternatively, the proportion of street trees in GOOD health does in fact differ between The Bronx and Manhattan. While I initially expected that Manhattan would have a higher proportion of healthier trees, the exploratory analysis revealed the opposite. To see the significance of this relation, I will use a one-side alternative hypothesis.

Hypothesis Notation
H₀ The proportion of trees in GOOD health is the same in The Bronx and Manhattan p₁ − p₂ = 0
Hₐ The proportion of trees in GOOD health is greater in The Bronx than Manhattan p₁ − p₂ > 0

Visualization of Statistical Significance

This graph illustrates that the null distribution is centered at 0, reflecting the assumption under the null hypothesis that there was no relation. The observed difference of 0.068, or 6.8 percentage points, is very far from the range of the null hypothesis. The shaded area is invisible with a p value of nearly 0, demonstrating that none of the 1000 permuted differences were as extreme as what we observed.

Results

I chose a significance level of α = 0.05, which is the conventional standard in social science. Practically, this means I require the probability of observing a difference as large as 6.8 percentage points by random chance alone to be less than 5% before rejecting the null hypothesis. Using this threshold, the p-value of less than 0.001 falls far below the threshold (0.05) required to fail to reject the null hypothesis, so I soundly reject it. This means that trees are definitively in better health in The Bronx than those in Manhattan, challenging the assumption that wealth corresponds to healthier streetscapes.

Discussion and Conclusion

The observed 6.8 percentage point difference, while being statistically significant, may not be as damning in the real world. The importance of this difference depends on context. The Bronx is home to almost 20,000 more trees than Manhattan, and is much less of a concrete jungle. Correspondingly, The Bronx has wider blocks and wider tree beds with more soil space, which leads to better tree health regardless of investment levels. On the other hand, Manhattan’s lower proportion of GOOD trees may reflect the stress than wide sidewalks, heavy foot traffic, and air pollution have on the borough’s trees.

This analysis set to find out whether street trees in The Bronx and Manhattan differ in health. Similar to the previous post, this was motivated by broader questions about green infrastructure equity in NYC and my passion for the topic. Using statistics and R, the answer was surprisingly clear—not in the extent to which they were different, but the direction. I found that The Bronx has a significantly larger proportion of trees in good health than Manhattan.

The bootstrap CI intervals from the beginning of the analysis fully agree with this conclusion, as they do not overlap at all. Further, the p-value of nearly 0, combined with the above difference, leads me to confidently reject the null hypothesis. That said, the context of both borough’s built environment, like land use patterns and tree species composition, is a factor that needs to be studied in future research.

item.title: Parks in NYC
item.dc:creator: Connor Wolosewicz
item.description:
Environmental Justice in NYC: There’s more than just Central Park.

Understanding New York City’s Parks

For this data analysis, I chose to investigate data on the New York City Park system. This covers parks that are managed partially or solely by NYC Parks, including what neighborhood and zip code the park belongs to, what kind of buildings you can find there, and how close it is to the waterfront. Each row is a property, with NYC Parks owning over 2,000 as of this year. You can find this dataset here.

I am interested in a birds-eye view of NYC’s park system, particularly to discover if parks are evenly distributed across the city’s neighborhoods. As someone who hopes to go into urban planning and making cities more sustainable in the face of climate change, this data is a great window into understanding NYC’s climate resilience and environmental justice.

Variables

I plan to hone in on several available variables, seen below.

Variable Type Description
ACRES Continuous Size of each park property in acres
BOROUGH Categorical NYC borough where the park is located (B = Brooklyn, M = Manhattan, Q = Queens, R = Staten Island, X = Bronx)
TYPECATEGORY Categorical Type of park (e.g., Neighborhood Park, Triangle/Plaza, Nature Area)

Data Cleaning and Statistics

For this dataset, I dropped all of the other variables, and filtered out rows where the ACRES value was missing. Also, I made sure that the BOROUGH values were spelled out correctly so that they are easier to interpret. Finally, my parks_clean dataset additionally changed BOROUGH and TYPECATEGORY, categorical variables, to factors, ensuring they are treated as such and not characters. This ultimately makes the data easier to analyze.

ACRES Visualization

The distribution of park size in NYC is strongly right-skewed and unimodal, with the vast majority of parks being under 2 acres, and the largest park being 2,772 acres. In regards to other measures, the median is 0.70 acres and the IQR is 1.89 acres, demonstrating that the typical NYC park is quite small. Outside of this range lies only 310 outliers, underscoring the trend of small parks; 179 were removed from the histogram for legibility.

BOROUGH and TYPECATEGORY Visualizations

The above bar charts illustrate a complex park system. The number of parks are distributed across the city’s boroughs fairly evenly, but there is a large gap between the greenest neighborhood (Brooklyn) and the least green (Staten Island). Specifically, Brooklyn has 463 more parks than Staten Island. Perhaps uniquely, gardens, playgrounds, neighborhood parks, and triangles/plazas are the most common types of public parks in NYC. Gardens in public parks is the most surprising to me, as I don’t see that often in DC, but I see plenty of triangles caused by our diagonal avenues.

Comparing Variables—Boroughs by Park Size

To understand my initial query on how park size varies by borough, I compared these two variables in a boxplot. Interestingly, while having the least amount of parks, Staten Island stands out with both the highest median park size and the most spread. On the other hand, the Bronx, Brooklyn, and Manhattan all have very similar and small median park sizes, suggesting that most NYC Parks properties in these boroughs are tiny pocket parks. Taken together, all five boroughs show lots of outliers above the whiskers, showing that a small number of very large parks are present across the city but are far from typical. Overall, this pattern makes sense given NYC’s density, as Staten Island is the least urbanized and has more room for larger green spaces.

Note—I again removed 179 entries for legibility.

item.title: Sydney Itinerary and Reflection
item.dc:creator: Connor Wolosewicz
item.description:
So you want to travel to Sydney?

If you’re looking to escape the cold, look no further than the land down under.

Last year, I studied abroad at the University of Sydney and lived in the city for 5 months. Follow along to hear the best 1-week itinerary that checks all of your boxes.

Day 1 — Check out the CBD

After checking into your hotel or Airbnb, take a walk along the water and admire the iconic Sydney Opera House. They have spectacular performances almost every day, and you can see what’s on the schedule here.

Day 2 — See the sunrise at Bondi

Take the 333 bus to Bondi Beach and walk along the gorgeous cliffs.

Day 3 — Dine out in Darlinghurst

This neighborhood is home to several mouth-watering spots.

Lunch

  • Small’s Deli
  • Piña
  • Cross Section

Dinner

  • Bills
  • Fabricca
  • Black Bottle

Day 4-5 — Go on a day trip

By train or car, you can make your way to several smaller destinations to explore more of Australia. Some of my favorites include:

  1. Blue Mountains
  2. Royal National Park
  3. Palm Beach
  4. Kiama

Important notes

Using data from Google, the average cost of living for a Sydneysider can get expensive. Here are some calculations to help you decide if this trip is right for you.

Cost of living for one week (in USD)

# Estimated costs of living for one week in Sydney
public_transit <- 30
groceries <- 100
phone_plan <- 10
rent <- 350
utilities <- 50
restaurants <- 50
entertainment <- 30
total_cost <- public_transit + groceries + phone_plan + rent + utilities + restaurants + entertainment
sprintf("$%.2f", total_cost)
[1] "$620.00"

DATS1001 Reflection

While I had already become familiar with R in my Scopes and Methods in Political Science course, that class was narrowly aimed at demonstrating trends or data in policy. As opposed to DATS1001, where I learned a better understanding of how coding and data analysis works in general. I think this was more helpful, as we actually worked through data cleaning and manipulation rather than going straight to illustration.

In particular, the most important thing I learned—especially in today’s age—was how machines learn. The k-nearest neighbors algorithm and its application to image classification tasks, where similarity is measured across pixel values, connects well to building AI systems. Now I have the intuition about why training data quality matters so much in AI: if the training images are unrepresentative, the nearest neighbors will lead the classifier astray. This helps me critically think about why or how an AI could produce systematically wrong predictions for underrepresented groups.
